deeper_dives.title: Physics Models → Market Alpha: When Scientists Become Traders
deeper_dives.thesis: 26 posts map physics/math models to trading — avalanche theory, Ising models, phase space reconstruction. The meta-insight: the quant edge isn't in finance, it's in importing models from other fields.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Wall Street's edge has always come from importing ideas other traders haven't seen. Renaissance Technologies hired physicists, not MBAs. This catalog maps the same playbook: Ising models from magnetism predict herd behavior, Omori's earthquake law models post-crash dynamics, phase space reconstruction from topology reveals hidden BTC patterns. The meta-insight isn't any single model — it's that the best alpha comes from fields nobody on Wall Street is reading. Each physics paper is a potential trading signal that quant Twitter hasn't priced in yet. The 26 posts here form a complete curriculum for building a cross-disciplinary signal ensemble.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Study the cross-disciplinary models. Build a signal ensemble that combines 3+ physics-derived indicators. Backtest with Monte Carlo validation. Deploy as an AI agent that runs daily.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Python + NumPy/SciPy, basic statistics, willingness to read physics papers, backtesting framework (backtrader or custom)

deeper_dives.title: Code as Canvas: When Algorithms Become the Artist
deeper_dives.thesis: Ant colonies, boid simulations, particle systems, and generative algorithms create art that's alive — emergent beauty from simple rules. The artist writes the rules, not the pixels.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Traditional art is about control — every brushstroke is intentional. Generative art inverts this: you write rules, and beauty emerges from complexity. Ant colony simulations create organic trail patterns. Boid flocking algorithms produce mesmerizing crowd dynamics. Particle systems generate physics-driven visual poetry. The artist's role shifts from pixel-pusher to system designer. The posts here show the full spectrum: from mathematical simulations to creative coding gardens to accidental beauty from music+code. This is art that's alive — it never produces the same output twice.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Pick a natural system (flocking, diffusion, growth). Code it in p5.js or TouchDesigner. Add interaction (sound, body tracking, data feeds). The art emerges — you don't draw it, you evolve it.

deeper_dives.title: Why Your Agent Sucks: Architecture Patterns That Actually Work
deeper_dives.thesis: Better models won't fix broken agent architecture. The gap between demo agents and production agents is context management, memory hierarchy, and harness engineering — not model capability.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Everyone's building agents. Almost nobody's building agents that work past the demo. The 103 new entries from this session surfaced a pattern: the posts that actually teach agent building focus on architecture — context management, memory hierarchies, harness engineering — not on which model to use. The context vs memory vs harness framework is the clearest mental model we've seen for why most agents fail: they dump everything into context, have no persistent memory, and lack structural guardrails. This dive connects the theory (architecture patterns) with the infrastructure (Onyx, MCP, Kimi) needed to implement it.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Audit your agent's context window usage → separate context (ephemeral) from memory (persistent) from harness (structural) → implement retrieval layer (Onyx or similar) → add tool-use via MCP → test with Claude Code as the orchestrator → measure task completion, not conversation quality.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Familiarity with LLM APIs, basic understanding of RAG. Claude Code or similar agent framework.

deeper_dives.title: Prompt Engineering Isn't Dead — It Just Grew Up
deeper_dives.thesis: Prompt engineering evolved from 'add please' to a real discipline: structured frameworks, adversarial testing, llms.txt standards, and system-level prompt architecture that shapes model behavior at scale.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The 'prompt engineering is dead' crowd missed the plot. It didn't die — it professionalized. Anthropic's own leaked system prompt shows structured instruction architecture. llms.txt is becoming a web standard. Google published a formal framework. The L1B3RT4S jailbreaks prove that prompt security is a real engineering discipline. And GitReverse shows you can reverse-engineer any output back to its prompt. This isn't 'ask nicely' anymore — it's systems engineering for language models.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Study Anthropic's leaked system prompt for structure patterns → implement llms.txt for your own sites → build a prompt library organized by use case (research, code, creative) → test prompts adversarially with L1B3RT4S techniques → add GitReverse to your workflow for learning from others' prompts.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Any LLM API access. Basic understanding of system vs user prompts.

deeper_dives.title: The Incentive Trap: Game Theory for the AI Age
deeper_dives.thesis: Game theory isn't abstract math — it's the operating system behind every AI product decision, every market move, and every platform play. The entries in this collection reveal the incentive structures hiding in plain sight.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The game-theory collection is new and small but punches above its weight. Elon's $1 is a masterclass in incentive design — the trap is always in the commitment structure, not the headline number. Combined with the AI origin story (decades of academic game theory producing today's landscape) and the physics-as-strategy entries, this collection reveals that every major decision has a game-theoretic structure. Understanding it is the difference between being a player and being played.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Map the incentive structure before making any decision → identify who benefits from each outcome → look for lock-in mechanisms (Elon's $1 pattern) → study failed strategies to understand what the 'game' actually is → apply physics intuition (equilibria, forces) to strategic situations.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: None — these are mental models, not technical implementations.

deeper_dives.title: The Claude Harness Pattern: Context, Memory, and Cognitive Scaffolding
deeper_dives.thesis: The most effective Claude users aren't writing better prompts — they're building harnesses: layered systems of CLAUDE.md files, skills, hooks, and memory that turn a chat window into a persistent, self-improving agent. This pattern emerged independently across multiple creators and maps directly to how production AI systems are being architected.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The gap between 'using Claude' and 'building with Claude' is exactly this pattern. Context engineering (what goes in the window), memory (what persists across sessions), and harness (the scaffold that orchestrates both) are three distinct disciplines. Most people conflate them. The entries in this dive show practitioners who figured out the distinction independently.
deeper_dives.action_sketch:
1. Audit your current Claude setup — do you have a CLAUDE.md? Skills? Hooks?
2. Map your workflow: what context does Claude need every session vs. occasionally?
3. Build a minimal harness: CLAUDE.md (permanent rules) + primer.md (session state) + skills/ (reusable workflows)
4. Add memory persistence: file-based or MCP-backed cross-session recall
5. Layer in hooks for self-correction (caveman mode, token discipline, audit-after-step)
prerequisites: Claude Code or similar agent-mode tool

deeper_dives.title: MediaPipe × TouchDesigner: Real-Time Body-to-Art Pipeline
deeper_dives.thesis: MediaPipe's body/hand/face tracking combined with TouchDesigner's node-based visual programming creates a real-time pipeline where human movement becomes generative art. Multiple creators are independently converging on this stack — from spider-web string simulations to 3D Gaussian splatting to interactive music visualizers.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: This is the democratization of interactive installation art. What used to require a team, custom hardware, and months of C++ now runs on a laptop with a webcam. The convergence of multiple creators on this exact stack signals it's become the de facto standard for real-time body-reactive art.
deeper_dives.action_sketch:
1. Install TouchDesigner + MediaPipe Python bindings
2. Set up basic hand landmark tracking (21 points per hand)
3. Pipe landmark data into TouchDesigner via OSC or shared memory
4. Build a simple particle system that responds to hand position
5. Layer in face mesh (468 landmarks) for expression-reactive visuals
6. Export as standalone for installations or stream via NDI
prerequisites: Basic understanding of node-based visual programming

deeper_dives.title: Beyond Prompting: Prompt Architecture as Engineering Discipline
deeper_dives.thesis: Prompt engineering is dead. What's replacing it is prompt architecture — the systematic design of context windows, memory layers, retrieval systems, and cognitive scaffolds. The shift from 'write a better prompt' to 'build a better system around the model' is the biggest unlock in practical AI right now.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Most people are still optimizing individual prompts. The practitioners in these entries have moved past that entirely — they're building systems where the prompt is just one component of a larger architecture. Context management, memory persistence, tool orchestration, and self-correction loops matter more than any single prompt.
deeper_dives.action_sketch:
1. Stop optimizing prompts in isolation — map the full context flow
2. Implement RAG with hybrid search + reranking for internal docs
3. Build a memory layer: what the model needs to remember across sessions
4. Add retrieval-augmented generation for domain-specific knowledge
5. Design self-correction: let the model audit its own output
6. Measure: track which context components actually improve output quality
prerequisites: Experience with at least one LLM API

deeper_dives.title: Autonomous Agents in the Wild: What's Actually Working in 2026
deeper_dives.thesis: Autonomous agents went from demo-ware to daily drivers in 2026. But the gap between 'impressive demo' and 'reliable tool' is massive. These entries map the frontier: Qwen agents roaming NYC streets, Claude automating daily workflows, Kronos failing hedge fund stress tests. The pattern: narrow agents with clear guardrails work; general agents with vague goals don't.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Everyone's building agents. Most are failing. The entries in this dive separate signal from noise: what agent patterns actually work in production, what fails under stress, and where the reliability boundary sits. The common thread: agents succeed when scope is narrow, feedback is fast, and failure is cheap.
deeper_dives.action_sketch:
1. Start narrow: one task, one tool, one feedback loop
2. Build in guardrails before capability (kill switches, budget limits, scope constraints)
3. Test adversarially: what happens when the agent encounters edge cases?
4. Add persistence: agents need memory to avoid repeating mistakes
5. Monitor: log everything, review regularly, prune bad patterns
6. Scale horizontally: multiple narrow agents > one general agent
prerequisites: Understanding of LLM capabilities and limits
Experience with at least one agent framework

deeper_dives.title: Art Without Gatekeepers: How AI is Rebuilding the Creative Economy
deeper_dives.thesis: The art market's gatekeeping structure — galleries, curators, collectors, degree requirements — is being dismantled by AI tools and direct distribution. Artists who adapt are finding new revenue streams (AI-assisted commissions, generative art NFTs, interactive installations). Those who resist are watching their distribution channels commoditize around them.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: For decades, art distribution required permission — from galleries, from collectors, from institutions. AI tools + direct platforms are removing that dependency. The entries show both the opportunity (new tools, new markets, new forms) and the risk (commoditization of traditional skills).
deeper_dives.action_sketch:
1. Inventory your skills: what do AI tools amplify vs. replace?
2. Experiment with AI-assisted workflow (not AI-generated — AI-assisted)
3. Build direct distribution: portfolio site, social presence, email list
4. Explore interactive/generative formats that galleries can't replicate
5. Price for the new market: commissions, prints, installations, licensing
6. Document your process — the behind-the-scenes is the new gallery wall
prerequisites: Existing art practice or strong visual sense
Willingness to experiment with AI tools

deeper_dives.title: Claude Mastery: From First Install to Advanced Agent Architecture
deeper_dives.thesis: Claude Code is not a chatbot — it's a programmable agent framework. The gap between 'using Claude' and 'mastering Claude' is a stack of learnable patterns: CLAUDE.md configuration, skills/hooks/MCP extensibility, memory persistence, cognitive scaffolding, prompt architecture, parallel worktrees, and multi-agent orchestration. This guide maps every layer with dated, sourced entries that track the evolution from Opus 4.6 through Fable 5.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Most Claude users are leaving 90% of its capability on the table. Skills, hooks, MCP servers, memory systems, and agent orchestration transform a conversational AI into a persistent, self-improving development partner. This is the definitive reference — living document that grows as Claude evolves.
deeper_dives.action_sketch:
1. Install Claude Code: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
2. Create CLAUDE.md in your project root with behavioral rules
3. Install 2-3 starter skills (brainstorming, systematic-debugging)
4. Set up auto-memory (file-based or MCP-backed)
5. Add hooks for session-start context loading
6. Learn thinking keywords (think → ultrathink progression)
7. Try parallel worktrees for multi-branch development
8. Build custom skills for your recurring workflows
